removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 13 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: drop with ajv – only used to pre-process schemas before Ajv; inline $ref resolution (~30 lines)
how_to_remove: Replace the single $RefParser.dereference() call in normaliseJsonSchema() with a small (~30-line) recursive function that walks the schema object, resolves internal $ref strings against the root schema (e.g. '#/definitions/Foo'), and substitutes them inline. No external resolution is needed (the existing call already passes {resolve: {external: false}}). After implementing and testing the inline resolver, remove the @apidevtools/json-schema-ref-parser dependency from packages/cli-kit/package.json and run pnpm install.
replacement_reason: The dependency is used for a single, narrow operation: in-memory dereferencing of internal $ref pointers with no external resolution. This is straightforwardly replaceable with a small recursive utility function, making the dependency unnecessary. Inlining also eliminates a transitive dependency chain and removes a large package from the bundle.

removal_summary: Remove – unused
how_to_remove: Remove the '@babel/core' entry from the 'dependencies' field in packages/eslint-plugin-cli/package.json, then remove it from the sharedDependencies array in packages/features/steps/node-deps.steps.ts and run pnpm install to update pnpm-lock.yaml.
replacement_reason: @babel/core is never imported or required at runtime; the sole string reference '@babel/no-unused-expressions' is an ESLint rule name that does not need @babel/core to be installed (it is satisfied by @babel/eslint-plugin if needed, or can simply be removed since the rule is already turned off).

removal_summary: Remove – unused
how_to_remove: Remove the explicit '@cucumber/messages': '30.1.0' entry from packages/features/package.json. The version will then be resolved transitively through @cucumber/cucumber. If the peer version requirement is already satisfied by @cucumber/cucumber's own dependency tree, the explicit pin is unnecessary. Verify compatibility with pnpm after removal.
replacement_reason: Not directly imported anywhere in source code. The explicit pin in package.json exists only to satisfy peer dependency resolution. Removing the explicit declaration and relying on the transitive resolution from @cucumber/cucumber is safe and reduces direct dependency surface.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 42 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Change the format entry in packages/features/cucumber.js from '@cucumber/pretty-formatter' to one of the built-in cucumber-js formatters such as 'progress' or 'summary', then remove the '@cucumber/pretty-formatter' entry from devDependencies in packages/features/package.json. Alternatively, '@cucumber/pretty-formatter' could be replaced with a different community formatter by swapping the string and dependency.
replacement_reason: The only usage is a single string in the cucumber.js config file selecting the output format. Switching to a built-in cucumber-js formatter (progress, summary, html, json) requires a one-line config change and removes the dependency entirely. The effort is trivial.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: replace with smol-toml – drop-in, actively maintained
how_to_remove: Replace the single import in codec.ts with a maintained alternative such as `smol-toml` (actively maintained, TOML 1.0 compliant, zero-dependency). The API surface needed is only parse(string) and stringify(object), both of which smol-toml provides. Steps: (1) add smol-toml to cli-kit dependencies, (2) update the import in codec.ts from `@iarna/toml` to `smol-toml`, (3) adjust the type cast on line 25 to use smol-toml's exported map type, (4) remove @iarna/toml from cli-kit/package.json and run pnpm install.
replacement_reason: @iarna/toml has had zero releases in the past 24 months (last release 2.2.5 was in 2019) and is effectively unmaintained. smol-toml is actively maintained, fully TOML 1.0 compliant, zero-dependency, and provides the exact same parse/stringify API surface that codec.ts needs.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace the import and usage of graphqlLoaderPlugin.default() in packages/app/src/cli/services/extensions/bundle.ts with an inline esbuild plugin. A graphql loader plugin is ~20 lines of code: an onLoad handler for the filter /\.graphql$/ that reads the file, wraps it in a gql template literal (or uses graphql-tag's gql), and exports the parsed DocumentNode. Update the two test mocks accordingly. Remove the dependency from packages/app/package.json and run pnpm install.
replacement_reason: The package has had zero releases in the last 24 months (last release was 3.8.1 on 2022-12-26, over 3 years ago) and shows no signs of active maintenance. The functionality it provides — an esbuild plugin that transforms .graphql files into importable JS modules — is straightforward (~20 lines) and can be inlined directly in bundle.ts using the graphql package already present in the dependency tree, eliminating the abandoned third-party dependency entirely.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 2 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: The plugin contributes only one command: 'commands'. To remove the dependency, implement a first-party replacement command in packages/cli/src/cli/commands/commands.ts that reads this.config.commands and renders a table (the existing cli-kit UI primitives already support this). The replacement logic is ~50–80 lines of TypeScript — straightforward given that the oclif implementation itself is only ~140 lines of compiled JS. Remove the import and the customPluginName iteration block from index.ts, remove the package from packages/cli/package.json, and add the new command to the COMMANDS map.
replacement_reason: The sole contribution is a single 'commands' listing command. This can be implemented in-house with negligible effort using cli-kit's existing renderTable/renderInfo primitives and the oclif Config.commands array that is already available at runtime. Removing it also eliminates an external dependency that injects its own lodash/object-treeify transitive dependencies.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 56 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: SemanticResourceAttributes.SERVICE_NAME is simply the string literal 'service.name'. It could be inlined as a string constant in DefaultMeterProvider.ts with a one-line change, eliminating this dependency in isolation without touching any other file. However, the broader OTel stack it supports still depends on the other five packages.
replacement_reason: The single used value (SERVICE_NAME = 'service.name') can be inlined as a string literal. Removing this package requires only a one-line change in DefaultMeterProvider.ts, making it the only member of the OTel group that is a genuine removal candidate on its own merits.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: inline – replace toBeInTheDocument() etc. with Vitest native assertions
how_to_remove: 1. Remove `@testing-library/jest-dom` from devDependencies in packages/ui-extensions-dev-console/package.json. 2. Remove the import line from packages/ui-extensions-dev-console/tests/setup.ts. 3. Replace any `toBeInTheDocument()` assertions in tests with standard Vitest alternatives: `not.toBeNull()` or checking `screen.queryByRole(...)` against null. A grep for `toBeInTheDocument` across the dev-console test files shows it is used in at least Extensions.test.tsx; a full search is recommended before removal. 4. Replace any other jest-dom matchers (toHaveTextContent, toHaveClass, toBeDisabled, etc.) found in the test files with equivalent Vitest DOM assertions or manual element property checks.
replacement_reason: The dependency is used only in a single setup file to extend Vitest matchers. The jest-dom matchers it provides (toBeInTheDocument, toHaveTextContent, etc.) can be replaced with native Vitest/DOM assertions at small effort. The scope is limited to one package and the changes are mechanical. Vitest itself has good built-in support for DOM assertions with jsdom, making the jest-dom extension layer unnecessary.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The 'brotli' package (devongovett/brotli.js) is a pure-JS port, last published in 2015 (v1.3.3). Node.js has had native brotli compression in its built-in 'zlib' module since v10.16.0 (via zlib.brotliCompress/zlib.brotliCompressSync). Replacing the import with Node's built-in zlib would eliminate both 'brotli' and '@types/brotli'. The call site is small (one compress call with quality/mode options). Effort is 'small': one production usage in archiver.ts and one test file to update.
replacement_reason: The 'brotli' package dependency (and thus @types/brotli) can be eliminated by switching to Node.js's built-in zlib.brotliCompress/brotliCompressSync, which requires no @types package and is available in all Node versions this project supports. This is a small refactor with a well-understood replacement.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 27 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: The 'commondir' package computes the common parent directory for an array of paths. It is a tiny, abandoned package (single 21-line function, last published ~2014). The functionality can be inlined as a small utility function in path.ts itself (no external dependency needed), or replaced with a modern alternative that ships types. Removing both 'commondir' and '@types/commondir' requires implementing ~5-10 lines of path logic in path.ts. Effort is 'small'.
replacement_reason: The 'commondir' package is a tiny, unmaintained (~2014) utility. Its functionality (finding the common parent of a list of paths) can be trivially inlined, eliminating the runtime dep and the @types package entirely. This also reduces supply-chain risk from an abandoned package.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 27 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: express@5 ships bundled TypeScript declarations. Upgrading express from 4.21.2 to express@5 would make @types/express redundant. The API surface used in server.ts (app.get, app.post, app.use, app.listen, express.Request, express.Response, bodyParser) is broadly compatible with express@5, so the migration effort is small (update import, adjust any breaking API changes, drop @types/express devDependency from app/package.json).
replacement_reason: express@5 ships bundled TypeScript declarations, eliminating the need for @types/express. Only one file in one package uses express types, making this a small migration.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: global-agent is last published at v3.0.0 (2021) and is effectively unmaintained. The functionality (bootstrapping a global HTTP/HTTPS proxy from environment variables such as GLOBAL_AGENT_HTTP_PROXY) is minimal — only 'createGlobalProxyAgent()' is called with no arguments in the CLI entry point. Replacement options: (1) switch to 'hpagent' or 'undici' proxy support configured at the fetch/http layer, or (2) use the 'proxy-from-env' + manual Agent wiring pattern. Given the single call site with no arguments, replacement effort is small. Removing both global-agent and @types/global-agent together is a small change.
replacement_reason: global-agent is unmaintained (last release 2021) and @types/global-agent mirrors a dead package. Replacing with an actively maintained proxy solution (e.g. hpagent) and dropping both runtime and @types dependencies is a small, targeted change at a single call site.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 2 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Remove '@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin' from the dependencies in packages/eslint-plugin-cli/package.json. No code changes required — the package will continue to be provided transitively via @shopify/eslint-plugin -> typescript-eslint -> @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin. Also update packages/features/steps/node-deps.steps.ts which lists '@typescript-eslint/parser' in sharedDependencies (a related cleanup). Run `pnpm install` and `pnpm run lint` to confirm nothing breaks.
replacement_reason: Already provided transitively through @shopify/eslint-plugin v50's dependency on the unified 'typescript-eslint' package. The direct declaration is redundant duplication.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 13 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: 1. Remove '@typescript-eslint/parser' from dependencies in packages/eslint-plugin-cli/package.json. 2. Remove '@typescript-eslint/parser' from the sharedDependencies array in packages/features/steps/node-deps.steps.ts (line 38) since this dep will no longer be directly declared. 3. Run `pnpm install` and `pnpm run lint` to confirm ESLint still works correctly via the transitive path.
replacement_reason: Already provided transitively through @shopify/eslint-plugin v50's dependency on the unified 'typescript-eslint' package. The direct declaration is redundant. The sharedDependencies version-check in the features integration test is a secondary concern that must be cleaned up alongside removal.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 52 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: inline – OSC 8 link is a 2-line function
how_to_remove: The single usage is ansiEscapes.link(label, url) which generates the string '\u001B]8;;{url}\u0007{label}\u001B]8;;\u0007'. This can be inlined as a one-liner utility function with no external dependency: (label, url) => `\u001B]8;;${url}\u0007${label}\u001B]8;;\u0007`. Remove the import from Link.tsx, add the inline helper, and remove the package from cli-kit's package.json.
replacement_reason: The only feature used is OSC 8 hyperlink generation, a trivially inlineable 2-line function. No external package is needed.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 16 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Replace the single line 'app.use(bodyParser.json())' with 'app.use(express.json())'. Remove the import 'import bodyParser from body-parser' from server.ts. Remove 'body-parser' from packages/app/package.json dependencies. express.json() is available in Express 4.x and is identical in behaviour.
replacement_reason: Express 4.x (already a declared dependency) provides express.json() which is body-parser's json() middleware re-exported. No additional package needed.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace with Node.js built-in zlib.brotliCompress (available since Node 10.16). The call is a single synchronous compression of a Buffer; the equivalent is `zlib.brotliCompressSync(tarContent, { params: { [zlib.constants.BROTLI_PARAM_QUALITY]: 7, [zlib.constants.BROTLI_PARAM_MODE]: zlib.constants.BROTLI_MODE_GENERIC } })`. Remove the `brotli` import and its @types package, update the integration test, and delete the dependency from cli-kit/package.json.
replacement_reason: Node.js >=10.16 ships zlib.brotliCompressSync which covers the exact call surface used here. The brotli npm package has had zero releases in the last 24 months (last published 2019), making it a maintenance liability. Migration is a single-site code change.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: absorb into change-case – inline helper using camelCase()
how_to_remove: Replace the two `camelcaseKeys(obj)` call sites with a small inline helper (10–15 lines) that recursively converts snake_case keys to camelCase using String.prototype.replace. Alternatively, vendor the single-function logic directly in the app-logs utilities. The `camelCase` function already available from the `change-case` package (declared in cli-kit and re-exported as `camelize`) can be used as the key transformer. Remove the `camelcase-keys` entry from app/package.json.
replacement_reason: The two call sites pass flat or single-level objects; shallow camelCase conversion is trivial to inline. The monorepo already has `change-case`'s `camelCase` function available via cli-kit's `camelize` export, so no new dependency is needed. camelcase-keys has had no releases in the past 24 months.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: absorb into chalk – inline JSON colorizer
how_to_remove: Replace the single call `cjs(stringifyMessage(this.value) ?? {})` in JsonContentToken.output() with a small inline coloriser using the already-present `colors` (ansi-colors / chalk) helper: pretty-print via JSON.stringify then apply color.cyan/yellow/green on keys, strings and numbers respectively, or simply use `JSON.stringify(..., null, 2)` with no coloring. The fallback already exists in the catch block. No callers need to change.
replacement_reason: The functionality (syntax-colored JSON for terminal output) can be replicated with a ~20-line inline helper using the ansi-colors/chalk instance already imported in the same package, eliminating an unmaintained single-purpose dependency.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: commondir's entire published source is ~15 lines. The one call site is `commondir([path, dir])` which returns the longest common path prefix. This can be inlined directly in relativizePath() using Node's built-in `path.resolve` and string splitting, or by importing `pathe` (already a direct dependency) utilities. No other file needs to change.
replacement_reason: The package is a tiny utility (< 20 lines) that has had no releases in years. The single call site in relativizePath() is trivially replaceable with an inline implementation using Node built-ins or the existing pathe dependency.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace the single call with the browser-native Clipboard API: `await navigator.clipboard.writeText(qrCodeURL)`. The component already uses React hooks (useCallback), so converting the handler to async and wrapping in a try/catch is a one-line change. Alternatively the Web Clipboard API is already available in Chromium-based Electron / browser contexts that ui-extensions-dev-console targets.
replacement_reason: The Web Clipboard API (navigator.clipboard.writeText) is a browser built-in that covers the single use case. copy-to-clipboard is only needed as a legacy fallback for IE/old Safari; the dev console does not need to support those environments.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 13 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: The single debug() call can be replaced by a conditional `if (process.env.DEBUG?.includes('eslint-plugin-cli')) console.error(...)` or simply removed, since it is a developer-facing diagnostic line inside an ESLint rule that most users never enable. No other rule files in the package use debug.
replacement_reason: The entire usage is one diagnostic log line in one ESLint rule file. Replacing it with a short inline conditional on process.env.DEBUG eliminates the dependency entirely with zero functional regression for end users.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: absorb into lodash – use lodash.merge or 15-line inline
how_to_remove: Replace the single `deepMerge(lhs, rhs, {arrayMerge})` call in object.ts with a small inline recursive merge function (~15 lines) or with structuredClone + a custom array-merge strategy. The surrounding `deepMergeObjects` signature stays unchanged, so no callers need updating.
replacement_reason: deepmerge has had zero releases since October 2022 (last version 4.3.1). The single usage is a trivial one-liner that is easily inlined. Removing it reduces supply-chain risk from an unmaintained package.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 58 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Replace `diffLines` with Node.js's built-in or a micro-implementation. A ~30-line Myers/line-diff function is sufficient given the narrow use-case (comparing two .env file strings). The `Change` type can be replaced with a local interface `{ value: string; added?: boolean; removed?: boolean }`. All three files would need updating, but the surface is narrow.
replacement_reason: Only one runtime call site (diffLines on .env file strings). The `Change` type is used in two other files but only as a TypeScript type annotation, replaceable with a local interface. The diff library is moderately active (~2 releases in 24 months) but the functionality needed is narrow enough to inline.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 10 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: The `parse` function maps `KEY=VALUE` lines to an object. Node 20.6+ ships `--env-file` flag support but no programmatic `parse` API. A ~20-line regex-based parser handling comments, quoted values, and multiline strings covers the .env spec subset the codebase already handles in patchEnvFile. Alternatively, the existing `patchEnvFile` parsing logic (already in dot-env.ts) could be refactored into a standalone `parse` equivalent.
replacement_reason: Only the `parse` function is used, and the file already contains hand-written .env line parsing logic in `patchEnvFile`. Consolidating into a shared inline parser removes the external dependency with minimal effort. dotenv is actively maintained (~5 releases in 24 months) but the narrow usage does not justify the dependency.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: env-paths resolves platform-specific directories: on macOS `~/Library/Caches/<name>` and `~/Library/Logs/<name>`, on Linux `~/.cache/<name>` and `~/.local/share/logs/<name>`, on Windows `%LOCALAPPDATA%/<name>/Cache` and `%LOCALAPPDATA%/<name>/Log`. A ~25-line inline function using `process.platform`, `os.homedir()`, and `process.env` (LOCALAPPDATA, XDG_CACHE_HOME, XDG_STATE_HOME) replaces it completely. Only two call sites need the `.cache` and `.log` properties.
replacement_reason: env-paths has had zero releases since January 2023 (last version 3.0.0) and is effectively unmaintained. The usage is limited to two property accesses (.cache, .log) for platform-specific directories. An inline implementation using os.homedir() and process.platform covers all three supported platforms with minimal code.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The @shopify/eslint-plugin already pulls in eslint-plugin-prettier and includes a .prettier config spread. Check whether shopifyPlugin.configs.prettier already includes eslint-config-prettier's rule-disabling config. If so, the explicit spread of eslintConfigPrettier is redundant and can be deleted along with the require() import and the package dependency. If not, the ~200 rule overrides it provides could be inlined as a rules object — tedious but straightforward. Either way, effort is small.
replacement_reason: @shopify/eslint-plugin's prettier config may already cover this; if not, the rules can be inlined or the dependency kept as it is a tiny zero-logic config package. Net effort to remove is small.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 14 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: keep – active, covers public API files
how_to_remove: Only one rule ('jsdoc/require-returns-description') is used. This rule enforces that @returns JSDoc tags include a description. It could be replaced by the tsdoc plugin's syntax rule (already present) or removed entirely if the team is comfortable relying only on TypeScript types. Alternatively the single rule check could be implemented as a small custom rule (~30 lines). Effort is small.
replacement_reason: Only one rule is used from a large plugin. The rule can be inlined as a custom rule or replaced by stricter tsdoc/syntax checks from the already-present eslint-plugin-tsdoc.

removal_summary: Remove – unused
how_to_remove: Delete the 'eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y' entry from the dependencies section of packages/eslint-plugin-cli/package.json and run pnpm install. No source code changes required.
replacement_reason: The package is declared but never used. It can be deleted with no code changes required.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The plugin exposes a single rule (no-catch-all). The functionality — disallowing bare catch(e) without type narrowing — overlaps significantly with the TypeScript ESLint rule '@typescript-eslint/use-unknown-in-catch-callback-variable' which is already declared in config.js but currently set to 'off'. Enabling that TS-eslint rule and removing this plugin would cover the same intent. Effort is small: flip 'off' to 'error' for '@typescript-eslint/use-unknown-in-catch-callback-variable', remove the plugin import and registration, remove the rule, and drop the package.json dependency.
replacement_reason: '@typescript-eslint/use-unknown-in-catch-callback-variable' (already a declared dependency, currently set to 'off') covers the same enforcement. Switching to it eliminates the external dependency with a one-line config change.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Drop the explicit 'eslint-plugin-prettier' entry from eslint-plugin-cli/package.json. The package will still be available transiently through @shopify/eslint-plugin (which requires it in its own prettier config). If @shopify/eslint-plugin ever stops bundling it, the 'prettier/prettier' rule would silently stop applying. Alternatively, switch to relying solely on eslint-config-prettier (already also used) which disables conflicting rules without running prettier as a lint rule — a pattern recommended by the prettier project itself.
replacement_reason: The explicit declaration is redundant since @shopify/eslint-plugin already pulls in eslint-plugin-prettier. Removing the direct dep reduces surface area without any functional change. If the team wants to drop prettier-as-a-lint-rule entirely, eslint-config-prettier (already present) is sufficient for disabling conflicting rules.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Remove the require() call and plugin registration from config.js, remove the 'unused-imports/no-unused-imports': 'error' rule, and remove from package.json. The equivalent functionality is already partially covered by '@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars' (already configured in baseRules lines 183-194 as an error). The specific unused-imports rule targets import declarations more precisely — the switch to @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars with 'varsIgnorePattern' and ensuring imports are included would cover the same ground. Alternatively, @typescript-eslint v7+ added 'no-unused-import' as a dedicated rule.
replacement_reason: The functionality (flagging unused imports) is already substantially covered by the existing '@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars' rule configured in config.js. The 'unused-imports/no-unused-imports' rule is additive but not unique. With @typescript-eslint already configured to catch unused vars including imports, removing this plugin represents trivial effort — verify no gaps by running lint before/after, and adjust @typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars config if any import-specific patterns need tuning.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 32 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: replace with Node http module – only used for 6-route GraphiQL server
how_to_remove: Replace with Node.js built-in http.createServer() plus manual routing (~6 routes), or use a minimal alternative like fastify or hono. The route count is very small (5 GET, 1 POST). The bodyParser.json() middleware can be replaced with JSON.parse on the readable body stream. express.Request/Response types would be replaced with http.IncomingMessage/ServerResponse. Estimated effort: ~100 lines of targeted rewriting in a single file, low risk.
replacement_reason: The usage is isolated to a single small server file with only 6 routes and no advanced express features. Node.js built-in http module is sufficient for this use case, eliminating a heavy dependency (express + body-parser) from the app package.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The 5 used symbols (tick ✔, cross ✖, square ▪, arrowUp ↑, arrowDown ↓) can be inlined as a small hand-rolled object in the figures.ts shim file, with the same Unicode fallbacks figures itself provides for Windows. Delete the import and replace with a plain const object. No other files need to change since they all import through the shim or via the package name (which resolves identically). Total change: ~10 lines in figures.ts.
replacement_reason: Only 5 Unicode symbols are used. These can be inlined trivially as a static object in the existing figures.ts shim, removing an external ESM-only dependency with no runtime benefit over a ~10-line inline constant.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Node.js has no built-in find-up equivalent, but the functionality is a simple recursive directory walk (check for file existence, move to parent, stop at root). A self-contained ~20-line async helper can replace find-up entirely within fs.ts, behind the existing findPathUp() wrapper. The public API stays unchanged. Alternatively, the locate-path or escalade packages are lighter options. Low risk since the call is already isolated behind a single wrapper function.
replacement_reason: The dependency is used only through a single wrapper function. Inlining a small recursive path-walker (~20 lines using fs/promises.access and path.dirname) eliminates the dependency with negligible effort and no API changes to callers.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 14 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: 1) Replace `FormData` from `form-data` with the global `FormData` available in Node 18+ (no import needed). 2) Replace `import {Headers} from 'form-data'` in api.ts with the global `Headers` type or a structural type `{forEach(cb: (v: string, k: string) => void): void}`. 3) Update the instanceof check in http.test.ts to use the global `FormData`. 4) Remove `form-data` from cli-kit's package.json. Scope: 3 files, ~5 line changes.
replacement_reason: Node 18+ ships both FormData and Headers as globals. The project already targets Node 18+ (evidenced by use of AbortSignal.timeout and fs/promises). Removing form-data eliminates a dependency whose only unique value was polyfilling these globals.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 56 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Replace with a small inline helper that opens a TCP server on port 0 (Node net module), records the assigned port, then closes the server — a well-known pattern that requires ~15 lines. The `checkPortAvailability` function can be replaced by attempting a connection on `net.createServer().listen(port, host)`. The wrapper in tcp.ts already isolates all calls behind `getAvailableTCPPort` and `checkPortAvailability`, so callers need no changes. Remove get-port-please from cli-kit's package.json.
replacement_reason: Node's built-in `net` module can fully provide random-port and port-check functionality. The existing comment in tcp.ts acknowledging a HOST env-var bug in the library is an additional motivation to remove the dependency.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: absorb into chalk – inline HSV gradient (~30 lines)
how_to_remove: Inline the gradient logic directly in TextAnimation.tsx using the 'chalk' package (already a dependency of cli-kit) and HSV-to-RGB conversion (a small pure function). The entire surface is a single private component with one call site; no public API is exposed. Alternatively, the feature could be removed entirely if the rainbow animation is cosmetic-only.
replacement_reason: chalk is already a declared dependency in cli-kit and supports 256-colour / truecolor ANSI output. A small HSV-to-RGB helper (~15 lines) plus chalk.rgb() calls would fully replace gradient-string's usage here. gradient-string itself has had zero releases in the last 24 months (latest pinned version is 2.0.2, which predates the window), indicating an unmaintained project.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The functionality (HTTP + WebSocket reverse proxying to a target URL) can be replaced with Node.js built-in http/https modules plus the 'http-proxy' package (the upstream from which http-proxy-node16 is forked), or with a lightweight alternative such as 'http-proxy-middleware' (express-compatible) or a manual pipe using http.request() and socket upgrades. The usage is isolated to one ~100-line file with a clear interface (createProxyServer, proxy.web, proxy.ws). http-proxy-node16 is a Node 16-compatibility fork of the original http-proxy; the upstream http-proxy package now supports modern Node versions and is a direct drop-in replacement.
replacement_reason: http-proxy-node16 is a maintenance fork created solely to fix Node 16 compatibility issues in the original http-proxy package. The upstream http-proxy package (v1.18+) now works on all current Node versions and has an identical API (createProxyServer, proxy.web, proxy.ws). Switching requires changing only the import string and the package.json entry. The package has had 0 releases in 24 months (locked at 1.0.6), confirming it is unmaintained.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: In github.test.ts, replace `import {isExecutable} from 'is-executable'` and the `isExecutable(targetPath)` call with the already-existing `fileHasExecutablePermissions` from '@shopify/cli-kit/node/fs' (which uses fs.access + X_OK internally). Remove is-executable from cli-kit/package.json dependencies. One-line change in one test file.
replacement_reason: The production equivalent (fileHasExecutablePermissions using fs.access/X_OK) already exists in cli-kit/src/public/node/fs.ts. The test simply needs to import that wrapper instead of the external package. Zero new code required.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Inline the check: replace `return isInteractive()` with `return Boolean(process.stdout.isTTY) && process.env.TERM !== 'dumb'` (or the equivalent two-liner). The is-interactive package source is ~10 lines. Remove the import and the dependency from package.json. The existing isTerminalInteractive() wrapper means the change is contained to one call-site in local.ts.
replacement_reason: The entire package is a ~10-line utility around process.stdout.isTTY and TERM env var checks. Node.js exposes both natively. Inlining the logic eliminates the external dependency with trivial effort.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Inline the WSL detection logic. The is-wsl package checks: (1) process.platform === 'linux', (2) the existence and content of /proc/version containing 'microsoft' or 'WSL'. This is a ~5-line inline implementation. Replace the dynamic import with a native fs.readFileSync('/proc/version') check guarded by process.platform === 'linux'. Remove is-wsl from package.json.
replacement_reason: The WSL detection algorithm is trivial and well-documented: check /proc/version for 'microsoft'/'WSL' on linux. The package itself is a thin wrapper around exactly that logic. Inlining it is a small, low-risk change confined to one function in system.ts.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 13 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Replace the single `jose.decodeJwt` call with a two-line inline decoder: the JWT payload is the base64url-encoded middle segment of the token, so `JSON.parse(Buffer.from(token.split('.')[1], 'base64url').toString()).sub` is equivalent. Remove the `import * as jose from 'jose'` import and the `"jose"` entry from cli-kit's package.json. No other files are affected.
replacement_reason: The only usage is decoding (not verifying) a JWT payload, which requires nothing more than a base64url decode plus JSON.parse — both available in Node.js built-ins (Buffer). The full jose library (JOSE standard: JWS, JWE, JWK, JWT) is massive overkill for this single decode-only call.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 2 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: replace with happy-dom – lighter weight, Vitest-recommended
how_to_remove: Switch the vitest `environment` from `'jsdom'` to `'happy-dom'` in both vite.config.mts files and replace `"jsdom": "^25.0.0"` with `"happy-dom"` in both package.json devDependencies. happy-dom is a lighter-weight DOM implementation that is the standard Vitest alternative. Some test helpers relying on jsdom-specific behaviour (e.g. exact error messages) may need minor adjustment.
replacement_reason: jsdom is only used as a Vitest test environment for DOM simulation in two React packages. happy-dom is a faster, maintained alternative that Vitest officially recommends and supports natively. The switch is mechanical (one config key per package) with minimal risk of test breakage.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace the `latestVersion(name)` call with a direct fetch to the npm registry: `fetch('https://registry.npmjs.org/' + name + '/latest').then(r => r.json()).then(d => d.version)`. This is a one-function change in node-package-manager.ts. Remove the `import latestVersion from 'latest-version'` statement and the `"latest-version": "7.0.0"` entry from cli-kit's package.json. Update the test mock to mock the fetch call instead.
replacement_reason: latest-version is a thin wrapper around a single npm registry fetch. Node.js 18+ ships `fetch` as a global built-in, making the dependency entirely redundant. The replacement is a one-liner fetch call.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Node.js's built-in `os.networkInterfaces()` returns all network interface details including MAC addresses. A two-line replacement: `Object.values(os.networkInterfaces()).flat().find(i => i && !i.internal && i.mac !== '00:00:00:00:00:00')?.mac ?? '00:00:00:00:00:00'`. This eliminates the dependency with no behavioral change. Alternatively the already-declared sibling dependency `network-interfaces` (v1.1.0 in package.json) can serve the same role.
replacement_reason: The single call `macaddress.one()` can be replaced with 2-3 lines using Node.js `os.networkInterfaces()` (built-in, no new dependency needed). macaddress@0.5.3 is pinned to an exact version and the npm package has seen very few releases historically, making it a low-value transitive dependency worth removing.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 58 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: replace with micromatch – already a transitive dep via fast-glob
how_to_remove: Option A: Switch to `micromatch` (already common in the Node.js ecosystem and used by many tools like fast-glob) — API is nearly identical. Option B: Since `fast-glob` is already a direct dependency in cli-kit, use `micromatch.isMatch()` which fast-glob itself bundles. Option C: Node.js 22+ provides `path.matchesGlob()` (stable in v22.10+) for simple cases, though it doesn't support the full minimatch feature set. Whichever replacement is chosen, only `fs.ts` and its tests need updating.
replacement_reason: Only one call site in fs.ts wraps minimatch. micromatch has a compatible API (`micromatch.isMatch(key, pattern)`) and is already indirectly present in the dependency tree via fast-glob. Alternatively, since this repo targets Node 20+, `path.matchesGlob` (available since Node 22.10) could be evaluated once Node version is bumped.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Option A: Replace with `mime` (mime@4) which is actively maintained and has a similar API: `import mime from 'mime'; mime.getType(fileName)`. The mutable `mimes` dictionary would need a small shim using `mime.define()`. Option B: Replace with `mime-types` (well-established, widely used). Option C: For the limited use case (extension -> MIME type lookup), inline a small static map of the MIME types actually needed by the codebase. Only `mimes.ts` and its consumers require changes.
replacement_reason: mrmime is pinned to 1.0.1 (an old version; current is 2.x). The usage is confined to a single 24-line file with two simple functions. The `mime` package (v4) is a well-maintained drop-in replacement with an equivalent lookup API. The mutable dictionary feature (`setMimeTypes`) maps cleanly to `mime.define()`.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Inline a one-line bigram helper directly in packages/plugin-did-you-mean/src/index.ts: `const bigram = (s: string) => Array.from({length: s.length - 1}, (_, i) => s.slice(i, i + 2))`. Remove the import and the `n-gram` entry from plugin-did-you-mean/package.json.
replacement_reason: The entire usage is a single `bigram(str)` call pattern that is trivially reproducible as a one-liner using Array.from. The package has had 0 releases in the last 24 months, indicating it is unmaintained. Inlining eliminates an external dependency with no functional risk.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace the `ni.fromIp(backendIp, ...)` call with a Node.js built-in equivalent using `os.networkInterfaces()`. Iterate its entries, flatten to IPv4 addresses, and check that `backendIp` is present among them. This is ~5–10 lines of code replacing the one call. Remove the `network-interfaces` entry from cli-kit/package.json and delete the co-located network-interfaces.d.ts stub.
replacement_reason: Node.js `os.networkInterfaces()` (available since Node 0.6, stable in all supported versions) exposes the same data that network-interfaces wraps. The package has 0 releases in 24 months and is effectively abandoned. A small inline replacement covers the single call site.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The repo already requires Node >=20.10.0 (cli-kit/package.json engines field). AbortController and AbortSignal are global built-ins in Node 20. Update packages/cli-kit/src/public/node/abort.ts to `export class AbortController extends globalThis.AbortController {}` and `export class AbortSignal extends globalThis.AbortSignal {}` (or remove the intermediate subclasses entirely and re-export globals directly). Remove the `node-abort-controller` dependency from cli-kit/package.json.
replacement_reason: The package was a polyfill for Node <15. The codebase now requires Node >=20.10.0, where AbortController and AbortSignal are stable globals. The polyfill is entirely redundant. The package has had 0 releases in the last 24 months. Removing it requires changing only one file (abort.ts) with no functional change to the 27 consumers.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace node-stream-zip in both test files with Node.js built-in zlib + fs (reading a zip for verification) or with the already-present 'archiver' package's streaming API, or use the lightweight 'unzipper' package. The tests only enumerate zip entries, so a trivial helper using Node's native `fs.readFileSync` + a pure-JS zip parser (e.g. `fflate`) would suffice. Two files, one call-site each.
replacement_reason: fflate is a zero-dependency, pure-JS zip reader/writer (ESM + CJS) that can replace the StreamZip entry-enumeration pattern in the two test files with minimal code change. Alternatively the tests could use the native Node.js `zlib` streams combined with `archiver`'s own reading helpers, keeping the dependency count flat.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The 'open' package is a thin cross-platform wrapper around `xdg-open` (Linux), `open` (macOS), and `start` (Windows). Replacing it requires ~20-30 lines of platform-detection logic using Node.js `child_process.spawn` and `process.platform`. This is well-understood, has no dependencies, and is a single function. Effort is small but not trivial because Windows shell escaping and error handling must be replicated correctly.
replacement_reason: The functionality (open a URL in the default browser) can be inlined as a small platform-switch using Node's built-in `child_process.spawn`. The 'open' package itself is essentially that, at ~150 lines. Given the single call site and well-documented platform behaviour, inlining removes one external dependency with minimal risk.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The single production use is formatting generated TypeScript source with options {parser: 'typescript', singleQuote: true}. This could be replaced by: (a) dropping formatting of generated types entirely (lowest effort, output is consumed programmatically), or (b) switching to a lighter alternative such as `dprint` (wasm-based, much smaller install) or simply calling the TypeScript compiler's printer API. Either path touches one wrapper file (file-formatter.ts) and one call site (ui_extension.ts). The eslint-plugin-cli devDependency can be removed without any source change.
replacement_reason: The sole production use is formatting auto-generated TypeScript that is not shown to users, so dropping formatting is the simplest path. If formatting must be preserved, dprint or @typescript-eslint/parser's built-in printer are lighter alternatives. The eslint-plugin-cli devDependency is already unused in source.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: The usage is a single `lockfile.lock()` / `releaseLock()` pair. Node.js 18+ provides `fs.openSync` with the `O_EXCL` flag for advisory locking on most platforms, but cross-platform reliability is limited. Lighter-weight alternatives include: (a) 'lockfile' (npm, minimal), (b) 'proper-lockfile' could be replaced by a manual stale-PID lock file using `fs.writeFileSync` + retry loop (~30 lines). Effort is small: one production file, one lock acquisition site.
replacement_reason: The locking pattern (write a PID file, retry on contention, auto-expire stale locks) is straightforward to implement with Node.js built-ins in ~30 lines. proper-lockfile itself has had no releases since 2020, meaning it is effectively unmaintained. Its last major version (4.x) is stable but carries risk for future Node.js compatibility. Inlining the lock logic removes the dependency entirely.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 1 dependabot bump
how_to_remove: Replace with the `qrcode` package (pure Node/browser, no React wrapper) and render the QR code into a or via a useEffect, or switch to the lighter `react-qr-code` (SVG-based, zero extra deps). The QRCodeModal component is self-contained (~100 LOC) so the change is isolated to that one file.
replacement_reason: react-qr-code is SVG-based, has no peer-deps beyond React, is actively maintained, and the API is a near-identical drop-in (). Alternatively, if the team wants to remove all wrapper libs, the bare `qrcode` package can generate a data-URL rendered in an tag with ~5 lines of useEffect.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 48 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: The entire routing setup is a BrowserRouter wrapping a single wildcard route. Remove the Routes.tsx wrapper entirely and render <Extensions /> directly. Because BrowserRouter is not used for navigation (the custom useNavigate hook bypasses it), removing it has no functional impact. Delete the Routes component and update its single call-site (likely App.tsx or main.tsx) to render Extensions directly.
replacement_reason: There is only one route (path='*') so no routing logic is actually needed. The custom useNavigate hook emits WebSocket events and does not depend on react-router-dom at all. The dependency adds ~25 kB to the dev-console bundle for zero routing benefit.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 1 dependabot bump
how_to_remove: Replace ToastContainer + toast() with a lightweight custom implementation: a React context holding a toast queue (array of {id, message}), a fixed-position container component that renders them with a CSS transition, and a dismiss-after-timeout effect. Alternatively use `sonner` (modern, tiny, framework-agnostic) which has the same imperative toast() API. Changes span 3 source files (~40 LOC total import sites) plus one new context/component.
replacement_reason: react-toastify v9/v10 carries a ~32 kB CSS bundle and ~15 kB JS. The dev-console only uses two toast() call-sites and a single ToastContainer. `sonner` is a smaller, modern alternative with the same imperative API, or the team can inline a ~60-line custom toast context since requirements are minimal (bottom-center, dark theme, no progress bar, close-on-click).

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Replace <TransitionGroup> in Modal.tsx with a React Fragment (it is only used as a thin wrapper to enable CSSTransition children). Replace <CSSTransition> in Dialog.tsx with a CSS-only approach: keep the enter/exit class names already defined in Dialog.module.scss and apply them directly via a local boolean state (isEntering/isExiting) toggled by useEffect. The animation logic is entirely CSS-driven; the library only adds/removes class names on mount/unmount. Approx 30–40 lines of React + hook code.
replacement_reason: The library's sole job here is toggling CSS class names on mount/unmount transitions. The same effect can be achieved with a small custom hook that applies the enter/exit classes and calls onClose after the transition ends, eliminating the dependency entirely.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 56 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Node.js does not have a built-in semver parser, but the usage is limited to three call sites: two SemVer.compare() calls and two satisfies(version, range) calls. These can be replaced with the lightweight `compare-versions` package (zero-dependency, ~800 bytes), or inlined with a simple regex-based comparator. The range syntax used is simple (>=x.y.z) so a custom implementation would be trivial. Estimated effort: ~20–40 lines or a swap to compare-versions.
replacement_reason: Only three call sites using basic comparison and a simple range check. compare-versions covers all these cases with zero transitive dependencies and a much smaller footprint than the full semver package.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: inline – single /\x1B\[[0-9;]*m/g regex
how_to_remove: The strip-ansi v7+ package is ESM-only and exports a single function that applies the regex /[\u001B\u009B][[\]()#;?]*(?:(?:(?:(?:;[-a-zA-Z\d\/#&.:=?%@~_]+)*|[a-zA-Z\d]+(?:;[-a-zA-Z\d\/#&.:=?%@~_]*)*)?)|(?:(?:\d{1,4}(?:;\d{0,4})*)?[\dA-PR-TZcf-nq-uy=><~]))/g to the input string. This one-liner can be inlined as a shared utility (e.g. in cli-kit/src/private/node/ansi.ts) and imported wherever needed. The e2e re-export wrapper already isolates the dependency surface.
replacement_reason: The entire package is a single regex applied to a string. Inlining the regex as a small utility function eliminates the dependency with zero behaviour change and removes the ESM interop wrapper currently needed in the e2e package.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: inline – 3-line env-var check; pairs with ansi-escapes
how_to_remove: Replace with a one-liner inline check using `process.env.TERM_PROGRAM` / `process.env.COLORTERM` heuristics, or inline the single boolean property that is used (`supportsHyperlinks.stdout`). The package itself is tiny (~50 lines); copying its logic into a local utility or using `ansi-escapes`'s own hyperlink support detection would be trivial. Only one production call site.
replacement_reason: The package is only used for a single boolean check (`supportsHyperlinks.stdout`). That logic can be inlined in a few lines using environment-variable heuristics (the same approach the package uses internally), removing one external dependency.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 13 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Both functions map directly to Node.js built-ins: `temporaryDirectory()` is equivalent to `fs.mkdtempSync(path.join(os.tmpdir(), ''))` and `temporaryDirectoryTask(cb)` is equivalent to creating a temp dir, calling the callback, then cleaning up in a finally block. Replace the two call sites in cli-kit/fs.ts and features/environment.steps.ts with inline Node.js code; remove the e2e package declaration. Total change is ~15 lines.
replacement_reason: Node.js `fs.mkdtemp` / `fs.mkdtempSync` combined with `os.tmpdir()` provide identical functionality with zero dependencies. The wrapper in cli-kit/fs.ts already abstracts the API, so callers would be unaffected by the swap.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
consolidation_note: redundant with ansi-escapes.link() – drop terminal-link
how_to_remove: Replace with a direct call to `ansiEscapes.link(text, url)` (already present in the codebase via the Link.tsx component) plus a manual terminal-support check (see supports-hyperlinks above). The fallback logic (5 lines) can be inlined. Only one production call site in content-tokens.ts.
replacement_reason: ansi-escapes is already used in the codebase (Link.tsx uses `ansiEscapes.link`) and covers the OSC 8 link generation. Inlining the fallback eliminates the terminal-link dependency entirely.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: ts-error's ExtendableError is a single-class shim that fixes the `instanceof` chain for Error subclasses in ES5 targets. Modern TypeScript with ES2015+ targets handles this natively. Replace the re-export in error.ts with a locally defined class: `export class ExtendableError extends Error { constructor(message?: string) { super(message); this.name = new.target.name; } }`. No downstream call sites need to change.
replacement_reason: The entire package provides one class (~10 lines) that is now unnecessary with ES2015+ targets. Inlining eliminates the dependency with a trivial one-time change in a single file.

removal_summary: Quick win – trivial removal, cuts 35 dependabot bumps
how_to_remove: Delete or replace packages/cli-kit/scripts/build-api-docs.js. If API docs are still needed, the script could be replaced with a simpler alternative (e.g. tsdoc + api-extractor) or the docs pipeline could be moved to a dedicated CI job that installs typedoc independently without it being a declared dependency of cli-kit.
replacement_reason: Used only in an optional doc-generation script, not in any production or test code. Removing it from the package.json dependency list has zero runtime impact.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, no upstream churn
how_to_remove: Node 18.19+ provides import('node:child_process').execFileSync with which-like PATH resolution, but a direct drop-in is `node:child_process` + manual PATH splitting. Alternatively the lightweight `locate-path` or a hand-rolled PATH scan using `fs.accessSync` could replace all three call sites. The total surface area is small — three call sites with well-understood semantics.
replacement_reason: All three usages (sync PATH lookup, find-all PATH lookup, nothrow PATH lookup) can be implemented with a small helper using Node's built-in `node:child_process` / `node:fs` / `process.env.PATH` without adding a new dependency.

removal_summary: Easy win – small effort, cuts 56 dependabot bumps
consolidation_note: inline – only yaml.stringify used; 3 call sites
how_to_remove: All three usages call only YAML.stringify() on simple objects. This can be replaced with a hand-rolled serialiser for the simple key-value / nested-object shapes involved, or by switching to the lighter-weight js-yaml (which is likely already a transitive dependency via @shopify/theme-check-node). Alternatively, a ~20-line recursive stringifier covers the object shapes used here.
replacement_reason: Only YAML.stringify is used, on simple object shapes, in a single file. js-yaml is already a common transitive dependency in the monorepo and exposes a compatible dump() API, making substitution a near-trivial find-and-replace.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Cannot be removed from packages/cli without also removing or replacing the @shopify/cli-hydrogen dependency (or upgrading it to a version that no longer relies on @ast-grep/napi). The dependency is a native binary (NAPI) used by cli-hydrogen's AST transformation utilities; replacing it would require either forking cli-hydrogen or waiting for an upstream change. If cli-hydrogen itself were removed, then @ast-grep/napi and the esbuild external entry in bundle.js could also be dropped.
replacement_reason: The dependency is actively used at runtime by @shopify/cli-hydrogen for AST code transformations. It is a native binary module that cannot be trivially replaced with a Node.js built-in. Removal would require changes to an upstream external dependency.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Replace the three Bugsnag-dependent functions with direct HTTP POST calls to the same Shopify-internal endpoints (https://error-analytics-production.shopifysvc.com). The payload format must match the Bugsnag Error Reporting API JSON schema. Stack-trace cleaning and metadata enrichment logic already lives in the surrounding code and can be reused. The rate-limiting and environment-guard logic is already independent of the Bugsnag client. Tests mocking @bugsnag/js in error-handler.test.ts would need to be rewritten to mock the new HTTP client instead. Estimated scope: replace ~80 lines of Bugsnag API calls with a lightweight fetch/http wrapper, update types, and update tests.
replacement_reason: The replacement would require implementing and validating a custom Bugsnag API payload builder against Shopify-internal relay endpoints. That is non-trivial due to the structured event envelope, session tracking hooks, and the need to exactly reproduce the on-error plugin mechanism used for stack-trace normalisation. The effort is classified as medium, so the feasibility rules indicate this is not a removal candidate at this time. If Shopify decides to migrate to a different error-monitoring service (e.g. Sentry) the effort could be re-evaluated, but a simple removal without a replacement would silently drop all production error reporting.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Would require replacing the entire BDD/acceptance test framework. All 8 step files and the world constructor would need to be rewritten using a different runner (e.g. Vitest, Jest, or a custom Node test harness). The .feature Gherkin files would either need to be removed or re-expressed as plain test specs. This is a full rewrite of the integration/acceptance test layer.
replacement_reason: The package is the core runtime for the entire acceptance test suite. Replacement effort is large and there is no drop-in alternative that would preserve the Gherkin .feature files without a major rewrite.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: 1. Define a local equivalent interface (e.g. in cli-kit's graphql.ts): 'interface TypedDocumentNode<TResult, TVariables> extends DocumentNode { __apiType?: (v: TVariables) => TResult }'. 2. Update the 8 hand-written API files to import this local type instead of @graphql-typed-document-node/core. 3. Update the graphql-codegen configuration (root package.json uses @graphql-codegen/typed-document-node 6.1.0) to use a custom scalar/plugin or the 'importDocumentNodeExternallyFrom' option pointing at the local definition, then regenerate all ~50 generated files. 4. Remove the dependency from packages/app/package.json and packages/cli-kit/package.json. Note: graphql-request's own .d.ts files import TypedDocumentNode from this package, so it will remain a transitive type dependency until graphql-request is upgraded to a version that bundles its own equivalent.
replacement_reason: The interface can be inlined locally (2-line definition), making an external package unnecessary. However the effort is 'medium' because ~50 auto-generated files import the type and the codegen pipeline (graphql-codegen config + @graphql-codegen/typed-document-node plugin) would need to be reconfigured to emit the local import path instead, followed by a full regeneration pass. The package has had zero releases in the last 24 months (pinned at 3.2.0 since 2022), meaning it is frozen/stable — it poses low maintenance risk and the removal pain exceeds the benefit at this time.

packages_declaring: app; cli-kit; cli; plugin-cloudflare; plugin-did-you-mean; theme
removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not feasible without replacing the entire CLI framework. @oclif/core provides the Command base class, flag/arg parsing, Config loading, Hook infrastructure, and the run/flush lifecycle. Every command across all six declaring packages would need to be rewritten against a different framework (e.g., commander, yargs, or a bespoke solution). The custom ShopifyConfig subclass, environment-flag merging in BaseCommand, and the fanout-hooks system in cli-kit are all tightly coupled to @oclif/core internals. A migration would be equivalent to a full CLI framework replacement — at minimum several engineer-months of work.
replacement_reason: @oclif/core is the foundational framework for the entire Shopify CLI. Removing it would require rebuilding the command dispatch, flag parsing, plugin loading, hook, and config subsystems from scratch. The coupling is pervasive across all major packages and test suites.

removal_summary: Keep – active upstream, hard to remove
how_to_remove: The package contributes the entire user-facing plugin management surface: install, uninstall, link, update, inspect, reset, and the update hook. Removing it entirely would mean either (a) dropping plugin management support from the CLI altogether, or (b) implementing these seven commands in-house. Option (a) is viable if the team determines that the existing user-installed-plugins feature (which allows end-users to extend the CLI at runtime) is not a supported use case — noting that the 'plugins' command is already hidden and 'plugins:install' has its description cleared, suggesting the team is actively de-emphasising this surface. Option (b) requires reimplementing npm/yarn package installation logic, the oclif plugin data-directory management, and the update hook — roughly a medium effort. If dropping: remove the import from index.ts, remove the hooks re-export, remove the package from packages/cli/package.json.
replacement_reason: Although the 'plugins' command is hidden and install description is cleared (signals of intentional de-emphasis), the package still contributes a hooks.update export that is re-exported from the cli package and consumed by the oclif lifecycle. Removing it risks breaking the CLI's update flow. The seven commands also represent non-trivial npm/plugin-management logic that would require meaningful reimplementation effort. This warrants a deliberate product decision before removal rather than a clean mechanical extraction, putting effort at 'medium' and the candidate at 'no' until the team confirms plugin management is no longer a supported feature.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Cannot be removed in isolation. The entire vendor/otel-js subtree (8 files: DefaultOtelService, DefaultMeterProvider, BaseOtelService, InstantaneousMetricReader, types, validators, throttle, utils) plus packages/cli-kit/src/private/node/otel-metrics.ts would need to be deleted or replaced. The otel-metrics.ts module is called from the CLI command lifecycle to record cli_commands_total, cli_commands_duration_ms, and cli_commands_wall_clock_elapsed_ms metrics to a Shopify OTLP collector endpoint. A full replacement would require either (a) removing metric telemetry entirely, (b) rewriting against a different metrics SDK, or (c) hand-rolling an OTLP HTTP exporter. All options require substantial cross-file changes.
replacement_reason: No lightweight drop-in replacement covers the full OTel metrics pipeline (MeterProvider + OTLP HTTP export + resource labelling). Removing the dependency requires removing or replacing the entire metrics subsystem, which is a deliberate product decision rather than a dependency hygiene task.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: ExportResultCode could theoretically be inlined as a local enum (trivial to replicate: SUCCESS=0, FAILED=1), but @opentelemetry/core cannot be removed without also removing the entire OTel stack because it is declared as a direct dependency coordinating with sdk-metrics internals. Any standalone extraction of ExportResultCode would still leave the rest of the stack intact. Full removal requires eliminating the entire metrics subsystem (same as @opentelemetry/api).
replacement_reason: Although the single imported symbol (ExportResultCode) could be inlined trivially, the surrounding OTel stack that makes it necessary cannot be removed without a larger architectural decision. Removing core in isolation while keeping sdk-metrics would cause peer-dependency conflicts.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Removing the OTLP HTTP exporter would require either (a) removing all metric export capability from the CLI, (b) hand-rolling a minimal OTLP/HTTP implementation using Node's built-in fetch/https, or (c) switching to a different metrics backend (e.g. statsd, Prometheus remote write). All options require rewriting DefaultMeterProvider.ts and otel-metrics.ts, and coordinating with the Shopify platform team about the collector endpoint.
replacement_reason: This package is the sole mechanism by which CLI metrics reach Shopify's OTLP collector. It cannot be removed without either ending metric reporting or replacing it with an equivalent transport.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: The Resource constructor call could technically be replaced by passing a plain object if MeterProvider's API allowed it, but the sdk-metrics MeterProvider requires an actual Resource instance. Removal requires eliminating the entire OTel stack, not just this one package.
replacement_reason: Tightly coupled to @opentelemetry/sdk-metrics MeterProvider constructor. Cannot be substituted without replacing the SDK itself.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: sdk-metrics is the foundation of the entire vendor/otel-js subtree. Removing it would require rewriting or deleting all 8 files in that subtree and the otel-metrics.ts private module. There is no smaller-footprint drop-in that provides MeterProvider + MetricReader + OTLP-compatible aggregation in Node.js.
replacement_reason: This is the central dependency the entire metrics subsystem is built on. Removal is only feasible as part of a complete removal or wholesale replacement of the CLI's OTel metrics pipeline.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: The entire e2e package is built around the Playwright runner. Replacing it would require migrating all test fixtures, custom CLI fixtures (cliFixture in setup/cli.ts), browser-login helpers, and spec files to a different end-to-end framework (e.g. Cypress, WebdriverIO). The browser-based login flow (packages/e2e/helpers/browser-login.ts) uses Playwright's Page API and browser automation features. All 7 spec files would need to be rewritten. The playwright.config.ts configuration (reporters, workers, retries, global timeouts) would need equivalent config in the replacement framework. This is a full framework swap with no trivial migration path.
replacement_reason: The effort is classified as large because @playwright/test IS the e2e test framework — every fixture, helper, and spec file depends on it. There is no way to remove it without replacing the entire e2e test suite. The dependency is actively maintained by Microsoft, has a healthy release cadence, and is the industry standard for browser-based end-to-end testing. Removal would eliminate all end-to-end test coverage.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: The dependency is central to the test-utils package's public API and used directly in multiple test files across three packages. Removal would require: 1. Replacing the two wrapper functions in ui-extensions-test-utils (render.tsx, renderHook.tsx) with implementations based on an alternative (e.g. Vitest's built-in component testing utilities or @testing-library/preact if applicable). 2. Updating all 5 direct usages in ui-extensions-dev-console tests to use the replacement API — the imports of `screen`, `fireEvent`, `render`, `act` would need to point elsewhere. 3. Updating the direct usage in ui-extensions-server-kit/ExtensionServerProvider.test.tsx. The @testing-library/react API (render, screen, fireEvent, act, renderHook) is deeply embedded in the test patterns across these packages. While Vitest does provide a jsdom environment, it does not ship its own render helpers — a replacement library would still be needed, making this a like-for-like swap rather than a true removal.
replacement_reason: The effort is classified as medium. The dependency is actively used across three packages with 8 direct import sites forming the backbone of React component and hook testing. There is no drop-in replacement available that would not require touching every test file. The library is healthy, actively maintained, and is the de-facto standard for React component testing. Removal without a replacement would leave the packages untestable at the component level.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: To remove, you would need to either: (1) replace the 'archiver' dependency with an alternative that ships bundled TypeScript types (e.g. 'archiver' v6+ if it ever adds types, or a different archiving library), or (2) write local ambient declarations in a .d.ts file within cli-kit. The archiver usage is non-trivial — it is used for both zip and tar+brotli compression across packages/cli-kit/src/public/node/archiver.ts — so swapping the underlying library requires validating the archiving API surface.
replacement_reason: The underlying 'archiver' package is actively used and ships no bundled types. @types/archiver is required for TypeScript compilation. Removal would break the build unless the underlying package is swapped for one that bundles types.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: To remove, you would need to: (1) replace 'body-parser' with a middleware that ships bundled types (e.g. express v5 includes its own body parsing, or use the built-in express.json()/express.urlencoded() which are already typed via @types/express), or (2) write local ambient declarations. In graphiql/server.ts, body-parser is used for JSON and URL-encoded body parsing via express middleware — this could be replaced with express's own built-in parsers, which would be a small refactor.
replacement_reason: The underlying 'body-parser' package is actively used and ships no bundled types. However, the functionality could be migrated to express's built-in body parsing (express.json()/express.urlencoded()), which would eliminate the need for both body-parser and @types/body-parser. That refactor is 'small' but out of scope for a pure types audit.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Cannot remove — @types/diff is actively needed because diff@5.x ships no bundled types. Removal would break type-checking in cli-kit/src/private/node/content-tokens.ts, cli-kit/src/public/node/output.ts, and app/src/cli/services/app/env/pull.ts. The only alternative is to migrate from 'diff' to a TypeScript-native library (e.g. 'diff-match-patch' or 'fast-diff' with bundled types), which is a medium-size refactor.
replacement_reason: diff@5.x has no bundled types; @types/diff is necessary for type-safe use of Change and diffLines across two packages.

removal_summary: Keep – active upstream, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Cannot remove — @types/fs-extra is actively needed because fs-extra@11.x ships no bundled types. Removal would break type-checking in cli-kit/src/public/node/fs.ts and two files in features. Possible long-term strategy: cli-kit already wraps fs-extra functions in its own public API (node/fs.ts), so fs-extra could be replaced by native Node.js fs/promises for those wrapped functions, eventually eliminating the fs-extra dependency entirely. The features package uses fs-extra directly for convenience; replacing with node:fs/promises is trivial per file but requires auditing all usage.
replacement_reason: fs-extra@11.x ships no bundled types and is actively used across two packages. Removal of @types/fs-extra would require first eliminating or replacing the fs-extra dependency itself.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not a removal candidate — still needed. If gradient-string ever ships bundled types or is replaced, remove @types/gradient-string from cli-kit devDependencies at the same time.
replacement_reason: gradient-string is actively used and does not bundle its own types. @types/gradient-string has had 0 releases in the last 24 months (the @types package is essentially stable/complete), but the underlying package is still in use so the type package must be retained.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not a removal candidate. To eliminate the dependency, replace all lodash usage with native JS equivalents or a typed-by-default alternative (e.g. es-toolkit). This would require touching ~20 source files across cli-kit.
replacement_reason: lodash is pervasively used and does not bundle types. Replacement would require a large refactor across cli-kit.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not removable — Node.js built-in APIs have no JS replacement. The version pin (18.x) could be updated to match the minimum Node engine (>=20.10.0). No removal path exists while the project uses Node.js built-ins.
replacement_reason: @types/node is essential for typing Node.js built-in modules and globals in the e2e package.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not a removal candidate while proper-lockfile is in use. If proper-lockfile is replaced (e.g. with a package that bundles types), remove @types/proper-lockfile from app devDependencies simultaneously.
replacement_reason: proper-lockfile is actively used in the app package build service and does not bundle its own types. The @types package had 0 releases in the last 24 months (stable/complete), but must be retained.

removal_summary: Keep – active upstream, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not removable while React is used. React does not bundle its own types (confirmed: react@19.2.4 package.json has no types field and no .d.ts files). @types/react must be retained in all five packages.
replacement_reason: React is the core UI framework used monorepo-wide. Neither react@18 nor react@19 bundles type declarations, so @types/react is essential across all five packages.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not removable while react-dom is used. react-dom does not bundle its own types. @types/react-dom must be retained in all three packages.
replacement_reason: react-dom is used for DOM rendering and server-side rendering in app and ui-extensions-dev-console. It does not bundle type declarations.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not a removal candidate while semver is in use. Alternatively, note that semver v7.x could be replaced with native Node.js 22+ semver support, but that would require a minimum Node version bump.
replacement_reason: semver is used for version comparison in cli-kit and does not bundle its own types. @types/semver must be retained.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not a removal candidate while which is in use. If which is replaced with a typed alternative, remove @types/which from both app and cli-kit devDependencies.
replacement_reason: which is actively used across app and cli-kit and does not bundle its own types. @types/which must be retained in both packages.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: Not a removal candidate while ws is in use. If ws is replaced with a WebSockets package that bundles types (e.g. uWebSockets.js, or the native WebSocket available in Node.js >= 22), @types/ws can be removed from app devDependencies.
replacement_reason: ws is actively used for WebSocket server functionality in the app package's extension dev server and does not bundle its own types.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: If removal is desired: replace with @vitejs/plugin-react-swc (uses SWC instead of Babel for faster transforms) as a drop-in replacement — change the import and package.json dep in both vite.config.mts files. Alternatively, if the React dependency for these packages is being removed entirely, the plugin can be dropped and the plugins array updated. Effort is 'small' because it is a 2-file change if swapping to SWC, or requires broader React removal work if eliminating React altogether.
replacement_reason: The package is actively imported and used in both vite configs for React JSX transformation. It cannot be removed without also removing React from these packages. If a speed improvement is desired, @vitejs/plugin-react-swc is a drop-in replacement using SWC instead of Babel, but it would be an upgrade not a removal.

packages_declaring: app; cli-kit; cli; create-app; plugin-cloudflare; plugin-did-you-mean; theme
removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
how_to_remove: To switch to the alternative v8 provider: 1. Change `provider: 'istanbul'` to `provider: 'v8'` in configurations/vite.config.ts. 2. Replace '@vitest/coverage-istanbul' with '@vitest/coverage-v8' in package.json and all seven package.json files. 3. Note that istanbul and v8 have different coverage semantics (v8 uses native V8 engine coverage, istanbul instruments source). If removal without replacement is desired, remove the entire coverage config block from vite.config.ts and remove the devDependencies — but that eliminates code coverage reporting.
replacement_reason: Actively required by the shared vitest/vite config for all test coverage runs. Cannot be removed without either switching providers (to @vitest/coverage-v8, which is a small migration) or disabling coverage entirely. The effort is 'small' for a provider swap, but the package itself is essential to the current setup.

removal_summary: Keep – stable, hard to remove
how_to_remove: If removal is desired: 1. Remove `const vitestPlugin = require('@vitest/eslint-plugin')` from config.js. 2. Remove the `vitest: vitestPlugin` entry from the plugins object. 3. Remove the four 'vitest/*' rules from baseRules in config.js. 4. Remove '@vitest/eslint-plugin' from package.json dependencies in eslint-plugin-cli. The four rules being removed would reduce test-file quality enforcement (consistent test naming, nesting limits, no skipped tests). Alternatively, replicate equivalent rules manually using custom rules in eslint-plugin-cli. Effort is 'small' — only config.js needs editing.
replacement_reason: Actively required and used to enforce vitest-specific linting rules across the entire monorepo. The rules it provides (consistent-test-it, max-nested-describe, no-disabled-tests, prefer-expect-resolves) are actively enforced as errors and represent intentional quality gates. Removing it would degrade test discipline enforcement with no equivalent replacement available.

removal_summary: Keep – high churn, hard to remove
